statement: Flokkur fólksins (the People's Party) occupies a contradictory position in the EU referendum debate. The party holds 8 seats in the Althingi and is a junior coalition partner alongside Samfylkingin (Social Democrats, pro-EU) and Viðreisn (Reform Party, strongly pro-EU). The coalition agreement includes the commitment to hold the 29 August 2026 referendum on resuming EU accession negotiations. However, Flokkur fólksins officially opposes EU membership and its leader Inga Sæland has repeatedly expressed EU-sceptic views. The party's voter base is split: Gallup data suggests roughly 35–40% of Flokkur fólksins voters support EU negotiations, while the majority oppose. This creates an unusual dynamic where a governing party is actively campaigning against the outcome its own coalition is facilitating. Similar situations have occurred in other European referenda — notably the 2016 UK referendum, where the Conservative cabinet was split between Leave and Remain camps.
source_name: Flokkur fólksins party platform; Gallup Þjóðarpúls cross-tabulations by party (February 2026); Althingi coalition agreement 2024
caveats: The specific voter split figures are approximate and based on polling cross-tabulations with relatively small subsamples. Flokkur fólksins's position could evolve as the referendum approaches. The party may be using the referendum as a way to express EU-scepticism while maintaining coalition stability. Internal party dynamics may not be fully visible in public polling.

related_entries.statement: Flokkur fólksins (People's Party) holds 8 Althingi seats in session 157 and is a coalition partner in the current government alongside Samfylkingin and Viðreisn. The party was founded in 2016 by Inga Sæland and is generally EU-sceptic, though it agreed to the coalition's referendum commitment. Current MPs: Inga Sæland (Reykjavíkurkjördæmi suður), Eyjólfur Ármannsson (Norðvesturkjördæmi), Ragnar Þór Ingólfsson (Reykjavíkurkjördæmi norður), Lilja Rafney Magnúsdóttir (Norðvesturkjördæmi), Kolbrún Áslaugar Baldursdóttir (Reykjavíkurkjördæmi suður), Sigurjón Þórðarson (Norðausturkjördæmi), Sigurður Helgi Pálmason (Suðurkjördæmi), Ásthildur Lóa Þórsdóttir (Suðurkjördæmi). Three Flokkur fólksins ministers serve in the cabinet: Eyjólfur Ármannsson (innviðaráðherra), Inga Sæland (félags- og húsnæðismálaráðherra), and Guðmundur Ingi Kristinsson (mennta- og barnamálaráðherra, outside minister).

After the 2013 Icelandic parliamentary elections, the new coalition government of the Progressive Party and Independence Party sought to withdraw Iceland's EU membership application. In February 2015, the governing parties submitted a bill to Althingi seeking approval to formally withdraw, but the bill failed due to vigorous opposition obstruction and was never put to a final vote. Foreign Minister Gunnar Bragi Sveinsson then unilaterally sent a letter to the EU on 12 March 2015, stating that Iceland should no longer be regarded as a candidate country. The EU's response was ambiguous — the European Commission's spokesperson stated that Sveinsson's letter did not constitute a formal withdrawal of the application. The Latvian EU presidency replied on behalf of the Council. The decision to bypass parliament provoked significant public protests outside the Althingi. The EU has continued to regard Iceland's 2009 application as technically still valid, a point that became relevant again in the lead-up to the 2026 referendum.

The Icelandic government announced in early 2026 that a national referendum on resuming EU accession negotiations would be held on 29 August 2026. The referendum is based on a parliamentary resolution (þingsályktunartillaga) submitted by the Foreign Minister. The three coalition parties — Samfylkingin (Social Democrats), Viðreisn (Reform Party), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party) — support holding the referendum. The referendum question concerns whether Iceland should resume the accession negotiations that were suspended in 2013, not whether Iceland should join the EU outright. This is an advisory referendum (ráðgefandi þjóðaratkvæðagreiðsla), as Icelandic constitutional law does not provide for binding referenda on policy questions (the only binding referendum mechanism is under Article 26 for presidential vetoes of legislation).

citations.report_title: 25. þáttur Silfursins: Stefnt að kosningu um ESB í sumar

claims.claim_text: Flokkur fólksins hefur alltaf verið hlynntur beinu lýðræði og Inga Sæland hefur talað fyrir þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslum alveg frá stofnun flokksins.
Það er fordæmalaust að ríki ætli að sækja um ESB-aðild þar sem ekki er meirihluti fyrir því á þingi og ekki einu sinni í ríkisstjórn.

citations.report_title: Enginn afsláttur gefinn segi þjóðin já við aðildarviðræðum

claims.claim_text: Allir flokkar ríkisstjórnarinnar voru sammála um að þjóðaratkvæðagreiðsla um ESB-aðildarviðræður yrði haldin, þrátt fyrir skiptar skoðanir innan og milli flokka.
